docs.title: Quay
docs.text:
Chrome DevTools with accessibility-tree semantics for browser automation.

Use your authenticated Chrome sessions with agent-browser-style element targeting.

docs.title: Why Quay?
docs.text:
Tool Your Sessions Accessibility Tree Performance agent-browser ❌ Fresh browser ✅ Full CDP tree + refs Fast Playwright ❌ Fresh browser ⚠️ Simplified snapshot Medium Chrome DevTools ✅ Your Chrome ❌ Raw protocol only Fast Quay ✅ Your Chrome ✅ Full CDP tree + refs Fast

Key advantage: Use your Gmail, banking, SSO sessions without re-authenticating. Full Chrome accessibility tree for semantic element targeting with ref IDs.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install quay

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
from quay import Browser

# Connect to your Chrome (must be running with --remote-debugging-port=9222)
browser = Browser()

# Navigate
browser.goto("https://github.com")
# Or: browser.navigate("https://github.com")

# Get accessibility tree (like agent-browser)
tree = browser.accessibility_tree()
print(tree.to_tree_str())

# Find elements by role
buttons = tree.find_by_role("button")

# Click by visible text
browser.click_by_text("Sign in")

# Type into focused element
browser.click_by_text("Email")
browser.type_text("user@example.com")

# Screenshot
browser.screenshot("/tmp/page.png")

# Wait for conditions
browser.wait_for(text="Welcome")
browser.wait_for_url(pattern=r"/dashboard")

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• ✅ Your authenticated sessions — Use Chrome you're already logged into
  • ✅ Accessibility tree — Full CDP accessibility tree with ref targeting
  • ✅ Click by text — Click elements by visible text
  • ✅ Wait conditions — wait_for(selector=), wait_for(text=), wait_for(url=)
  • ✅ Multi-tab support — List, create, activate, close tabs
  • ✅ Recording & playback — Record sessions for replay
  • ✅ Screenshot comparison — Basic byte-by-byte comparison
  • ✅ Automatic reconnection — Recovers from WebSocket disconnects
  • ✅ Type hints — Full type hint coverage
  • ✅ Python 3.10+ — Modern async/await

docs.title: Code Review — Round 6 (v0.4.x Enterprise Features)
docs.text: Date: 2026-03-24 Files Reviewed: browser.py, models.py, connection.py, pyproject.toml, test files Focus: v0.4.0 features (PDF generation, multi-tab coordination, recording/playback, screenshot comparison)

docs.title: 1. Recording fidelity broken — _record_depth scope issue in compound async methods
docs.text:
File: browser.py \u2192 wait_for_load_state()

Compound async methods use this pattern:

try:
    self._record_depth += 1
    return self._run_async(_wait())  # _wait() may still have pending CDP calls
finally:
    self._record_depth -= 1  # Decremented BEFORE async _wait() loop completes

The finally runs when _wait() returns, but _wait() is an async loop that may still be executing CDP operations. When those CDP calls complete and trigger _record_action(), _record_depth is already 0, so the guard not _record_depth > 0 evaluates to True and recording proceeds.

Impact: Compound actions like goto and wait_for_load_state record their internal CDP steps as separate actions. A single goto("https://example.com") in a recording may produce multiple sub-actions (new_tab, accessibility tree, load state). Playback then replays each sub-action with its own timing and no compound semantics, breaking recording fidelity.

Fix approach: Remove the _record_depth guard entirely. The _playing_back flag already prevents re-recording during playback. Alternatively, scope _record_depth to truly synchronous call-stack depth, not async operation boundaries.

docs.title: 2. close_tab() leaves stale _current_tab reference
docs.text:
File: browser.py \u2192 close_tab()

if tab_id:
    self._http_put(f"/json/close/{tab_id}")
    if self._pool:
        self._run_async(self._pool.remove(tab_id))
    return True

If the closed tab was self._current_tab, the reference is never cleared. Subsequent calls to _get_current_tab() return the stale closed tab. Should clear the reference when closing the active tab:

if tab_id == self._current_tab?.id:
    self._current_tab = None

docs.title: 3. click(double=True) — parameter accepted but silently ignored
docs.text:
File: browser.py \u2192 click()

def click(self, ref: str, ..., double: bool = False, ...) -> bool:
    self._record_action("click", ref=ref, ..., double=double, ...)
    # `double` and `button` are recorded but never passed to _click_async
    return self._click_async(ref, tab=tab, timeout=timeout)

_click_async() always fires a single left-click. Playback of click(double=True) would do a single click. The parameter should either work or not be recorded.

docs.title: 4. PIL imported inside update_baseline conditional block
docs.text:
File: browser.py \u2192 assert_visual_match()

if update_baseline:
    shutil.copy(current_path, baseline)
    from PIL import Image  # <- Only imported if update_baseline=True

If Pillow is not installed and update_baseline=True, this raises ModuleNotFoundError inside an exception handler, producing a confusing traceback. Should be imported at the top of the function (alongside the existing numpy import guard) or with a clear error message.

docs.title: 5. wait_for_navigation() ignores tab parameter
docs.text:
File: browser.py \u2192 wait_for_navigation()

def wait_for_navigation(self, timeout: float = 10.0, wait_until: str = "load") -> bool:
    return self.wait_for_load_state(state=wait_until, timeout=timeout)

The docstring and method signature include tab: Tab | None = None, but it is not passed to wait_for_load_state. Calling browser.wait_for_navigation(tab=some_tab) waits on the current tab, not some_tab.

docs.title: 6. Recording.from_dict() silently drops path field
docs.text:
File: models.py \u2192 Recording.from_dict()

@classmethod
def from_dict(cls, data: dict) -> Recording:
    actions = [Action.from_dict(a) for a in data.get("actions", [])]
    return cls(
        actions=actions,
        version=data.get("version", "1.0"),
        # 'path' field from data is never restored
    )

The path field is not restored from saved recordings. If you load a recording with Recording.from_dict() and call save(), it writes to a temp file. Minor — not how the API is typically used.

docs.title: 7. threshold parameter has no range validation
docs.text:
File: browser.py \u2192 compare_screenshots()

def compare_screenshots(self, baseline: str, current: str,
                        threshold: float = 0.0, ...) -> ComparisonResult:

threshold accepts any float. Negative values silently work (all comparisons return False), and values > 100 always return match=True. Should validate 0.0 <= threshold <= 100.0:

if not 0.0 <= threshold <= 100.0:
    raise ValueError("threshold must be between 0.0 and 100.0")

docs.title: 8. Reconnect failure loses original CDP error context
docs.text:
File: browser.py \u2192 _send_cdp()

success = await conn.reconnect(...)
if not success:
    raise ConnectionError("Failed to reconnect...")  # Original error not included

If reconnect fails, the original CDP error (the reason reconnect was needed) is lost. The ConnectionError only says "failed to reconnect" without indicating which command failed or why. Should include the method name and/or preserve the original error.

docs.title: 9. temp_tab context manager lets close_tab exceptions propagate
docs.text:
File: browser.py \u2192 temp_tab()

@contextmanager
def temp_tab(self, url: str = "about:blank", close_on_exit: bool = True):
    tab = self.new_tab(url)
    try:
        yield tab
    finally:
        if close_on_exit:
            self.close_tab(tab)  # Exceptions here propagate to caller

If close_tab raises (e.g., _http_put fails), the exception propagates out of the context manager. This may be unexpected if the workflow succeeded but cleanup failed. Consider wrapping and logging instead of propagating cleanup failures.

docs.title: 10. to_tree_str() — format parameter shadows built-in
docs.text:
File: models.py \u2192 AXNode.to_tree_str()

def to_tree_str(self, indent: int = 0, format: str = "text") -> str:

format shadows Python's built-in. Minor style issue — fmt or output_format would be clearer.

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text:
All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.

The format is based on Keep a Changelog, and this project adheres to Semantic Versioning.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• Fix 01: ConnectionPool eviction closes WebSocket — _evict_oldest() now properly awaits conn.disconnect() instead of just marking disconnected, preventing socket file descriptor leaks.
  • Fix 05: Screenshot comparison documentation — Clarified byte-level comparison limitations, removed misleading threshold usage, added note about Pillow for pixel-level diffing.
  • Fix 07: Wait methods use async evaluate — Added _evaluate_async() for use from async contexts. Fixed RuntimeError when sync evaluate() called from within _poll_until().
  • Fix 08: Eager WeakKeyDictionary init — _enabled_domains now initialized via _init_cdp_mixin() called from BrowserCoreMixin.__init__, preventing race conditions in concurrent access.
  • Fix 09: find_by_name word-boundary matching — Added exact and interactive_only parameters. Substring matches now use word boundaries (e.g., "Sign" no longer matches "Design"). click_by_text defaults to interactive_only=True.
  • Fix 10: type_text clears content — Added clear=True parameter (default). Now sends Ctrl+A + Backspace before typing, matching Playwright's fill() behavior for intuitive replacement.
  • Fix 12: CLI context manager — All CLI commands now use browser_context() context manager for proper Browser cleanup and connection pool release.
  • Fix 13: Consistent CDP parsing — Fixed wait_for_load_state() to use correct result.result.value nesting, matching evaluate() and get_html().

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• CRITICAL: evaluate() return value parsing — CDP Runtime.evaluate returns {"result": {"result": {"value": ...}}}. The wrong nesting level caused evaluate() to always return None. Now correctly extracts result.result.value.
  • HIGH: click_by_text(0,0) fallback removed — When an AXNode has no bounding box (e.g., <title>, <meta>), the code silently clicked at viewport origin (0,0), causing unpredictable behavior. Now raises BrowserError with helpful message.
  • Issue #6: Exception swallowing without logging — All except Exception: pass blocks now log at DEBUG level for observability. WebSocket close errors, message parsing errors, callback errors, and health check failures are now logged.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• DES-1: wait_for* methods now use async polling (asyncio.sleep) instead of blocking time.sleep
  • DES-2: _evict_oldest no longer uses fire-and-forget asyncio.create_task
  • DES-3: Age-based cleanup for stale pending futures (30s threshold)
  • DES-4: CDP domain caching prevents redundant Domain.enable calls
  • DES-5: get_existing() now checks connection state before returning
  • DES-6: Recording playback uses action whitelist for security

docs.title: Install Package
docs.text: pip install quay

docs.title: Enable Chrome Remote Debugging
docs.text: Quay connects to Chrome via the Chrome DevTools Protocol. You must start Chrome with remote debugging enabled.

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
/Applications/Google\ Chrome.app/Contents/MacOS/Google\ Chrome --remote-debugging-port=9222 &

Or create a shortcut:

# Add to ~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc
alias chrome-debug='/Applications/Google\ Chrome.app/Contents/MacOS/Google\ Chrome --remote-debugging-port=9222'

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
google-chrome --remote-debugging-port=9222 &

# Or chromium
chromium --remote-debugging-port=9222 &

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: "C:\Program Files\Google\Chrome\Application\chrome.exe" --remote-debugging-port=9222

docs.title: Verify Connection
docs.text:
from quay import Browser

browser = Browser()
tabs = browser.list_tabs()
print(f"Connected! Found {len(tabs)} tabs")
browser.close()

docs.title: Development Install
docs.text:
For contributing or local development:

git clone https://github.com/sidonsoft/quay
cd quay
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Optional Dependencies
docs.text:
The core package has minimal dependencies:

  • websockets>=12.0 — WebSocket client for CDP
  • pyyaml>=6.0 — Configuration parsing

Development dependencies:

pip install -e ".[dev]"  # Includes pytest, ruff, mypy

docs.title: "No Chrome found"
docs.text: Make sure Chrome is running with --remote-debugging-port=9222.

docs.title: "Connection refused"
docs.text:
Check that nothing else is using port 9222:

lsof -i :9222

docs.title: "Multiple Chrome instances"
docs.text:
If you see "Chrome is already running", close all Chrome windows and restart with the flag:

pkill -f Chrome
chrome --remote-debugging-port=9222 &

docs.title: 1. Connect to Chrome
docs.text:
from quay import Browser

browser = Browser()

Chrome must be running with --remote-debugging-port=9222. See Installation.

docs.title: 2. Navigate Pages
docs.text:
# Open a new tab and navigate
tab = browser.new_tab("https://example.com")

# Navigate current tab
browser.navigate("https://github.com")

# Or use goto (creates tab + navigates + waits)
tab = browser.goto("https://example.com")

# Wait for page load
browser.wait_for_load_state("load")

docs.title: 3. Use the Accessibility Tree
docs.text:
tree = browser.accessibility_tree()
print(tree.to_tree_str())

# Find elements by role
links = tree.find_by_role("link")
buttons = tree.find_by_role("button")

# Search by name (case-insensitive substring)
submit = tree.find_by_name("Submit")

# Find by ref ID
node = tree.find("42")

# All interactive elements
interactive = tree.find_interactive()

docs.title: 4. Interact with Pages
docs.text:
# Click by visible text
browser.click_by_text("Sign in")
browser.click_by_text("Submit")

# Double-click
browser.click_by_text("Edit", double=True)

# Right-click
browser.click_by_text("Options", button="right")

# Type into the focused element
browser.click_by_text("Search")
browser.type_text("hello world")

# Type slowly
browser.type_text("slow typing", slowly=True)

# Execute JavaScript
browser.evaluate("document.querySelector('#btn').click()")

docs.title: 5. Wait for Conditions
docs.text:
# Wait for element
browser.wait_for(selector="#results")

# Wait for text
browser.wait_for(text="Welcome back")

# Wait for exact URL
browser.wait_for_url(url="https://example.com/dashboard")

# Wait for URL pattern (regex)
browser.wait_for_url(pattern=r"/users/\d+")

# Wait for element visible
browser.wait_for_selector_visible("#modal")

# Wait for element hidden
browser.wait_for_selector_hidden("#loading")

# Wait for JS condition
browser.wait_for_function("window.dataLoaded === true")

# Wait for page load
browser.wait_for_load_state("load")

docs.title: 6. Extract Content
docs.text:
# Page HTML
html = browser.get_html()

# JavaScript results
title = browser.evaluate("document.title")
url = browser.evaluate("window.location.href")

# Screenshot
browser.screenshot("/tmp/page.png")
browser.screenshot("/tmp/full.png", full_page=True)

docs.title: 7. Work with Tabs
docs.text:
# List tabs
tabs = browser.list_tabs()

# New tab
new_tab = browser.new_tab("https://github.com")

# Switch tabs (returns previous)
previous = browser.switch_to_tab(new_tab)
browser.switch_to_tab(previous)

# Temporary tab (auto-closes)
with browser.temp_tab("https://example.com") as tab:
    tree = browser.accessibility_tree()

# Close tab
browser.close_tab(new_tab)

docs.title: 8. Record and Replay
docs.text:
browser.start_recording()

browser.goto("https://example.com")
browser.click_by_text("Login")
browser.type_text("user@example.com")

path = browser.stop_recording()
browser.playback(path, speed=2.0, verify=True)

docs.title: 9. Handle Disconnections
docs.text:
# Default: auto-reconnect enabled
browser = Browser()

# Customize
browser = Browser(
    reconnect=True,
    reconnect_max_retries=5,
    reconnect_backoff=0.5,
    reconnect_callback=lambda msg: print(f"Reconnection: {msg}"),
)

# Check connection
if browser.is_connected():
    print("Ready")

docs.title: 10. Error Handling
docs.text:
from quay.errors import BrowserError, ConnectionError, TimeoutError

try:
    browser.goto("https://example.com")
except ConnectionError as e:
    print(f"Connection failed: {e}")
    print(f"Details: {e.context}")  # Dict with url, tab_id, etc.
except TimeoutError as e:
    print(f"Operation timed out after {e.timeout}s")
except BrowserError as e:
    print(f"Browser error: {e}")

docs.title: 11. Advanced: Direct CDP Access
docs.text:
# Send raw CDP commands
result = browser.send_cdp(
    "Page.navigate",
    {"url": "https://example.com"}
)

# Low-level connection access
conn = await browser._get_connection(tab)
await conn.send("Runtime.evaluate", {"expression": "1 + 1"})

docs.title: Architecture Decision Records
docs.text: This directory contains Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) for Quay.

docs.title: What is an ADR?
docs.text:
An ADR is a document that captures an important architectural decision, including:

  • Context — The issue we are facing
  • Decision — What we decided
  • Consequences — Trade-offs, benefits, and downsides

docs.title: ADR Index
docs.text: ADR Title Status 0001 Accessibility Tree for Element Targeting Accepted 0002 Runtime.evaluate Result Structure Accepted 0003 Runtime.callFunctionOn this Binding Accepted 0004 Accessibility Node Backend DOM ID Field Accepted 0005 Nested Async Function Handling Accepted

docs.title: Creating a New ADR
docs.text:
1. Copy the template and fill in the sections.
  2. Update this index.

docs.title: Template
docs.text:
# ADR-NNNN: Title

## Status
Proposed | Accepted | Deprecated | Superseded

## Context
What is the issue?

## Decision
What did we decide?

## Consequences
What becomes easier or harder?

docs.title: ADR-0001: Accessibility Tree for Element Targeting
Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Browser automation tools typically use CSS selectors or XPath:

# Playwright approach
page.click('button[data-testid="submit"]')

# Selenium approach
driver.find_element(By.CSS_SELECTOR, '.submit-btn')

Problems with CSS selectors: 1. Brittle — Frontend changes break selectors 2. Framework-specific — React/Vue/Angular generate different class names 3. Dynamic IDs — Changes between sessions 4. No semantic meaning

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
We use the accessibility tree for element targeting:

tree = browser.accessibility_tree()
print(tree.to_tree_str())
# - RootWebArea "GitHub" [ref=1]
#   - link "Sign in" [ref=42]
#   - textbox "Email" [ref=43]
#   - button "Submit" [ref=44]

browser.click("42")
browser.click_by_text("Sign in")
browser.type_by_name("Email", "user@example.com")

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Stable across frontend changes
  • Framework-agnostic
  • Human-readable tree output
  • Matches user perspective

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Requires Chrome
  • Extra CDP call to fetch tree
  • Ref IDs change after navigation

docs.text:

  • agent-browser

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
CDP Runtime.evaluate returns results in a nested structure:

{
  "result": {
    "result": {
      "type": "string",
      "value": "actual value here"
    }
  }
}

The double nesting (result.result.value) is confusing.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
We handle the full CDP response structure explicitly:

def evaluate(self, script: str) -> Any:
    result = self._send_cdp("Runtime.evaluate", {
        "expression": script,
        "returnByValue": True,
    })
    if "exceptionDetails" in result:
        raise CDPError(...)
    inner = result.get("result", {})
    value = inner.get("result", {}).get("value")
    return value

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Correct behavior matching CDP specification
  • Clear error handling

docs.title: Negative
docs.text: • Confusing API for users expecting simple structure

docs.title: References
docs.text: • CDP Runtime.evaluate

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted (v0.2.6 bug fix)

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
CDP documentation states: "The object is passed as this to the function."

Many implementations incorrectly pass the element as an argument:

# WRONG
self._send_cdp("Runtime.callFunctionOn", {
    "functionDeclaration": "(element) => element.click()",
    "arguments": [{"objectId": object_id}],  # WRONG
})

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
The target object is bound to this, NOT passed as argument:

# CORRECT
self._send_cdp("Runtime.callFunctionOn", {
    "functionDeclaration": "function() { this.click(); }",
    "objectId": object_id,
    "returnByValue": True,
})

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Correct behavior following CDP specification
  • Simpler arguments

docs.title: Negative
docs.text: • Confusing API for JavaScript developers unfamiliar with CDP

docs.title: References
docs.text: • CDP Runtime.callFunctionOn

docs.title: ADR-0004: Accessibility Node Backend DOM ID Field
Status
docs.text: Accepted (v0.2.6 bug fix)

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
CDP specification defines the field as backendDOMNodeId:

{
  "backendDOMNodeId": 123
}

Common mistakes: - backendNodeId — shortened version (incorrect) - backend_node_id — Python convention (incorrect) - backendDOMNodeId — CDP specification (correct)

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Always use backendDOMNodeId exactly as defined in CDP:

backend_node_id = node.get("backendDOMNodeId")  # Correct

result = await self._send_cdp("DOM.resolveNode", {
    "backendNodeId": backend_node_id,  # Note: DOM.resolveNode uses backendNodeId
})

Note: DOM.resolveNode uses backendNodeId but accessibility nodes use backendDOMNodeId.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• CDP Accessibility.Node
  • CDP DOM.resolveNode

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Accepted (v0.2.6 bug fix)

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Python coroutines and Tasks are different types:

# Coroutine
coro = my_func()  # <coroutine object>

# Task
task = asyncio.create_task(my_func())  # <Task>

When checking return values, it is easy to confuse them:

# WRONG: Returns coroutine, not bool
if check():  # Always truthy
    ...

# CORRECT: Await first
result = await check()
if result:
    ...

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Always handle async return values correctly:

# CORRECT: Await before checking
async def _execute_script(self, script: str) -> bool:
    try:
        result = await self._send_cdp(...)  # Await
        return bool(result)
    except Exception:
        return False

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Correct boolean checks
  • Prevents silent bugs

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Verbosity — need intermediate variable
  • Easy mistake — forgetting await

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Python Asyncio Documentation
  • PEP 492 — Coroutines

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
from quay.errors import BrowserError, ConnectionError, TimeoutError, CDPError

try:
    browser.navigate("https://example.com")
except ConnectionError as e:
    print(f"Chrome not reachable: {e}")
    print(f"Context: {e.context}")  # Dict with url, tab_id, etc.
except TimeoutError as e:
    print(f"Timed out after {e.timeout}s: {e.operation}")
except CDPError as e:
    print(f"CDP protocol error: {e}")
except BrowserError as e:
    print(f"Browser error: {e}")

See Error Handling for full error hierarchy.

try:

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Quay provides a custom exception hierarchy.

docs.title: Exception Hierarchy
docs.text:
BrowserError (base)
├── ConnectionError   # WebSocket connection issues
├── TimeoutError      # Operation timeout
├── TabError          # Tab management errors
└── CDPError          # Chrome DevTools Protocol errors

docs.title: BrowserError
docs.text:
Base exception for all Quay errors.

from quay.errors import BrowserError

try:
    browser.navigate("https://example.com")
except BrowserError as e:
    print(f"Browser error: {e}")

docs.title: ConnectionError
docs.text:
Raised when WebSocket connection fails.

from quay.errors import ConnectionError

try:
    browser = Browser()
    browser.navigate("https://example.com")
except ConnectionError as e:
    print(f"Cannot connect to Chrome: {e}")
    print("Make sure Chrome is running with --remote-debugging-port=9222")

docs.title: TimeoutError
docs.text:
Raised when an operation exceeds its timeout.

from quay.errors import TimeoutError

try:
    browser.wait_for(text="Done", timeout=5.0)
except TimeoutError as e:
    print(f"Timed out waiting: {e}")

docs.title: TabError
docs.text:
Raised for tab management errors.

from quay.errors import TabError

try:
    browser.close_tab(tab)
except TabError as e:
    print(f"Tab error: {e}")

docs.title: CDPError
docs.text:
Raised for Chrome DevTools Protocol errors.

from quay.errors import CDPError

try:
    browser.evaluate("invalid javascript")
except CDPError as e:
    print(f"CDP error: {e}")
    print(f"Error code: {e.code}")
    print(f"Error message: {e.message}")

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Always close browser in finally
browser = Browser()
try:
    browser.navigate("https://example.com")
finally:
    browser.close()
  1. Handle specific errors
try:
    browser.wait_for(text="Done")
except TimeoutError:
    print("Timeout")
except ConnectionError:
    print("Disconnected")

docs.title: Benchmark Guide
docs.text: quay is optimized for low-latency, real-world interactions. The benchmark suite compares quay against common automation tools.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
# Install quay in editable mode
pip install -e .

# Install competitors
pip install playwright && playwright install chromium
pip install selenium

Your Chrome instance must be running with --remote-debugging-port=9222.

docs.title: Running Benchmarks
docs.text:
python -m benchmarks.run_benchmarks

Options: - --scenarios: Filter specific scenarios - --runs: Number of iterations per tool (default: 10) - --results-dir: Custom directory for JSON results - --output: Format (table, json, markdown)

docs.title: Available Scenarios
docs.text: Scenario Description navigation Cold navigation to simple and JS-heavy pages accessibility_tree Time to fetch and parse the semantic AX tree click_performance Latency of text-based and ref-based clicks form_fill Speed of typing and filling multiple complex forms screenshot Time to capture both viewport and full-page screenshots

docs.title: Comparison Tool
docs.text:
cd quay
python benchmarks/compare.py results/*.json

docs.title: Adding New Scenarios
docs.text:
1. Create a new file in benchmarks/scenarios/.
  2. Inherit from BaseScenario.
  3. Implement the run() and teardown() methods.
  4. Add the scenario to the SCENARIOS registry.
from benchmarks.scenarios.base import BaseScenario

class CustomScenario(BaseScenario):
    def run(self, browser, page_url):
        with self.timer("total_interaction"):
            browser.click_by_text("Special Interaction")

docs.title: Interpreting Results
docs.text:
• mean_ms: Average latency. Focus on this for overall responsiveness.
  • p95_ms: 95th percentile latency. Critical for identifying jitter.
  • success_rate: Reliability measure. Ensure tools are completing tasks.

docs.title: Cookies
docs.text:
⚠️ Planned — Not Yet Implemented

The cookie management API (get_cookies, set_cookies, delete_cookies) is planned for a future release. This page documents the intended design. Track progress: Roadmap

docs.title: Cookies
docs.text: Manage browser cookies for authentication and session handling.

docs.title: Get Cookies
docs.text:
# Get all cookies
cookies = browser.get_cookies()
for cookie in cookies:
    print(f"{cookie['name']}: {cookie['value']}")

# Get cookies for a specific URL
cookies = browser.get_cookies(urls=["https://example.com"])

docs.title: Set Cookies
docs.text:
# Set a single cookie
browser.set_cookies([{
    "name": "session",
    "value": "abc123",
    "domain": ".example.com"
}])

# Set multiple cookies
browser.set_cookies([
    {"name": "token", "value": "xyz789", "domain": ".example.com"},
    {"name": "preferences", "value": "{\"theme\":\"dark\"}", "domain": ".example.com"}
])

# Set with full options
browser.set_cookies([{
    "name": "session",
    "value": "abc123",
    "domain": ".example.com",
    "path": "/",
    "secure": True,
    "httpOnly": True,
    "expires": 1735689600
}])

docs.title: Delete Cookies
docs.text:
# Delete specific cookie
browser.delete_cookies("session")

# Delete all cookies
browser.delete_cookies()

docs.title: Use Case: Transfer Session
docs.text:
# Login in one browser
browser = Browser()
browser.navigate("https://example.com/login")
browser.fill_form({"Username": "user", "Password": "pass"})
browser.press_key("Enter")

# Extract session cookies
cookies = browser.get_cookies()
session_cookies = [c for c in cookies if "session" in c["name"]]

# Transfer to another browser instance
browser2 = Browser()
browser2.new_tab("https://example.com")
browser2.set_cookies(session_cookies)
# Now browser2 is logged in!

docs.title: Use Case: Restore Session
docs.text:
import json

# Save cookies
cookies = browser.get_cookies()
with open("cookies.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(cookies, f)

# Later... restore cookies
with open("cookies.json", "r") as f:
    cookies = json.load(f)
browser.set_cookies(cookies)

Press a key or key combination:

# Single key

# Key combinations
browser.press_key("a", modifiers=["Control"])  # Ctrl+A (select all)
browser.press_key("c", modifiers=["Control"])  # Ctrl+C (copy)

browser.press_key("s", modifiers=["Control"])  # Ctrl+S (save)

Type text character by character with delay:

# Type with default delay (0.05s per character)
browser.type_slowly("email-input", "user@example.com")

browser.type_slowly("search-box", "query", delay=0.01)

# Type slower (like human)

docs.text:
Type text instantly into an element:

# Type by accessibility ref
browser.type_text("42", "Hello")

# Type into focused element
browser.press_key("Tab")
browser.type_text(None, "some text")  # Uses focused element

docs.text:
Click an element:

# Click by accessibility ref
browser.click("42")

# Click by text
browser.click_by_text("Submit")

# Double-click
browser.click("42", double=True)

# Right-click
browser.click("42", button="right")

# Click with specific tab
browser.click("42", tab=tab)

docs.text:
Hover over an element:

# Hover by accessibility ref
browser.hover("42")

docs.text:
# Fill form fields
browser.fill_form({
    "Name": "John Doe",
    "Email": "john@example.com",
    "Password": "secret123"
})

# Submit form
browser.press_key("Enter")

# Or click submit button

docs.text:
# Navigate with Tab
for i in range(5):
    browser.press_key("Tab")

# Select from dropdown
browser.press_key("ArrowDown")
browser.press_key("ArrowDown")
browser.press_key("Enter")

docs.title: Drag and Drop
docs.text:
Currently not directly supported. Use JavaScript:

browser.evaluate('''
const source = document.querySelector("#item");
const target = document.querySelector("#drop-zone");
source.dispatchEvent(new DragEvent("dragstart"));
target.dispatchEvent(new DragEvent("drop"));
''')

docs.title: File Upload
docs.text:
# Use type_text with file input
browser.type_text("file-input", "/path/to/file.txt")

docs.title: Multi-Tab Workflows
docs.text: Quay supports coordinated multi-tab operations for complex automation scenarios.

docs.title: Isolated Workflows with temp_tab()
docs.text:
The temp_tab() context manager is the recommended way to handle isolated, throwaway workflows. It ensures that tabs are cleaned up automatically after use.

from quay import Browser

with Browser() as browser:
    with browser.temp_tab("https://www.google.com") as tab:
        browser.type_by_name("Search", "python browser automation")
        browser.press_key("Enter")
        browser.wait_for_load_state()

        # Extract data from the isolated tab
        results = browser.get_html()
        print(f"Collected {len(results)} characters of HTML")

    # Tab is automatically closed here!

docs.title: Context Switching with switch_to_tab()
docs.text:
When working with multiple long-lived tabs, switch_to_tab() allows you to jump between them while tracking the previous state.

# Create two working tabs
tab1 = browser.goto("https://github.com")
tab2 = browser.goto("https://news.ycombinator.com")

# Switch to GitHub
previous = browser.switch_to_tab(tab1)

# Do some work...
browser.click_by_text("Sign in")

# Switch back to HN
browser.switch_to_tab(previous)

docs.title: Parallel Data Collection (Sequential)
docs.text:
urls = ["https://site-a.com", "https://site-b.com"]
data = []

for url in urls:
    with browser.temp_tab(url) as tab:
        browser.wait_for_load_state()
        data.append({
            "url": url,
            "title": tab.title,
            "tree": browser.accessibility_tree().to_dict()
        })

docs.title: Monitoring Background Changes
docs.text:
with browser.temp_tab("https://dashboard.example.com", close_on_exit=False) as dashboard:
    # Do work in main tab...
    browser.goto("https://task-manager.com")

    # Briefly check dashboard
    browser.switch_to_tab(dashboard)
    if "Alert" in browser.get_html():
        print("Dashboard needs attention!")

    browser.activate_tab(main_tab_id)

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Always use temp_tab() for secondary tasks to ensure your browser does not end up with dozens of stale tabs.
  2. Explicitly pass tab to specific methods if you are doing complex multi-tab logic.
  3. Use close_on_exit=False sparingly when you need to inspect the final state of a tab after a complex interaction.

docs.title: Network Interception
docs.text:
⚠️ Planned — Not Yet Implemented

The network interception API (on_request, on_response, on_request_failed) is planned for a future release. This page documents the intended design. Track progress: Roadmap

docs.title: Network Interception
docs.text: Monitor and intercept HTTP requests and responses.

docs.title: Enable Network Monitoring
docs.text:
# Network interception requires enabling Network domain
browser = Browser()
tab = browser.new_tab("https://example.com")

# Network domain is automatically enabled when you use on_request/on_response

docs.title: Monitor Requests
docs.text:
# Log all requests
def log_request(request):
    print(f"-> {request['request']['method']} {request['request']['url']}")

browser.on_request(log_request)

# Filter specific requests
def log_api_calls(request):
    url = request['request']['url']
    if '/api/' in url:
        print(f"API call: {url}")

browser.on_request(log_api_calls)

docs.title: Monitor Responses
docs.text:
# Log all responses
def log_response(response):
    status = response['response']['status']
    url = response['response']['url']
    print(f"<- {status} {url}")

browser.on_response(log_response)

# Filter by status code
def log_errors(response):
    status = response['response']['status']
    if status >= 400:
        print(f"Error: {status} {response['response']['url']}")

browser.on_response(log_errors)

docs.title: Monitor Failed Requests
docs.text:
# Log failed requests (network errors, timeouts)
def log_failed(request):
    url = request['request']['url']
    error = request.get('errorText', 'Unknown error')
    print(f"X Failed: {url} - {error}")

browser.on_request_failed(log_failed)

docs.title: Use Case: Track API Calls
docs.text:
api_calls = []

def track_api(response):
    url = response['response']['url']
    if '/api/' in url:
        api_calls.append({
            'url': url,
            'status': response['response']['status'],
            'type': response['response'].get('mimeType', '')
        })

browser.on_response(track_api)

# Navigate and interact...

print(f"Made {len(api_calls)} API calls:")
for call in api_calls:
    print(f"  {call['status']} {call['url']}")

docs.title: Use Case: Wait for XHR
docs.text:
import time

pending_requests = set()

def track_start(request):
    request_id = request['requestId']
    pending_requests.add(request_id)

def track_end(response):
    request_id = response['requestId']
    pending_requests.discard(request_id)

browser.on_request(track_start)
browser.on_response(track_end)

# Wait for all XHR to complete
while pending_requests:
    time.sleep(0.1)

docs.title: Note
docs.text: Network interception is for monitoring only. To modify requests, you need a proxy or use Chrome extensions.

docs.title: Automatic Reconnection
docs.text: Quay automatically recovers from WebSocket disconnections.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
When the WebSocket connection to Chrome drops:

  1. Quay detects the disconnection
  2. Background task attempts to reconnect
  3. Operations are queued during reconnection
  4. Queued operations execute after connection restores
  5. Operations fail if max retries exceeded

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
from quay import Browser

# Default (reconnect enabled)
browser = Browser()

# Disable auto-reconnection
browser = Browser(reconnect=False)

# Customize reconnection
browser = Browser(
    reconnect=True,
    reconnect_max_retries=5,
    reconnect_backoff=0.5,
)

# With callback for status updates
def on_reconnect(message):
    print(f"Reconnection: {message}")

browser = Browser(
    reconnect=True,
    reconnect_callback=on_reconnect
)

docs.title: During Reconnection
docs.text:
# Operations queue during disconnect
browser.navigate("https://example.com")
browser.click("42")

# Operations execute after reconnection

docs.title: Connection State
docs.text:
from quay.connection import ConnectionState

state = browser.state
if state == ConnectionState.CONNECTED:
    print("Ready to go")
elif state == ConnectionState.DISCONNECTED:
    print("Reconnecting...")
elif state == ConnectionState.RECONNECTING:
    print("Attempting to reconnect")

docs.title: Reconnection Backoff
docs.text:
Exponential backoff: Wait time = base_backoff * (2 ^ attempt_number)

- Attempt 1: 0.5s
- Attempt 2: 1.0s
- Attempt 3: 2.0s
- Attempt 4: 4.0s
- Attempt 5: 8.0s

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use callbacks for monitoring
def log_reconnect(msg):
    logger.warning(f"Reconnection: {msg}")

browser = Browser(reconnect_callback=log_reconnect)
  1. Set appropriate max_retries
# Quick fail for short tasks
browser = Browser(reconnect_max_retries=3)

# More patient for long tasks
browser = Browser(reconnect_max_retries=10)

docs.title: Session Recording and Playback
docs.text:
Quay supports recording and replaying browser sessions for testing, debugging, and automation.

⚠️ Known Issues

See Changelog for current bugs and limitations. Playback validates actions against a whitelist of allowed operations for security.

docs.title: Recording a Session
docs.text:
To record a session, use start_recording() and stop_recording(). Actions performed during this period are captured and serialized to JSON.

from quay import Browser

browser = Browser()
browser.start_recording("login_flow.json")

browser.goto("https://example.com/login")
browser.type_by_name("username", "user@example.com")
browser.type_by_name("password", "secret")
browser.click_by_text("Sign In")

browser.stop_recording()  # Saves to login_flow.json

docs.title: Playing Back
docs.text:
To replay a previously recorded session, use playback().

from quay import Browser

browser = Browser()
# Replay at double speed with verification enabled
browser.playback("login_flow.json", speed=2.0, verify=True)

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text: Use Case Benefit Regression testing Replay user journeys after UI changes to ensure they still work. Bug reports Share a JSON recording with developers to provide perfectly reproducible steps. Demo automation Record a complex demo once and replay it reliably on demand. CI/CD integration Automate complex login or navigation flows without writing code for every step.

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
You can pause and resume recording if you need to perform actions that should not be captured.

browser.start_recording("workflow.json")

browser.goto("https://example.com/start")
browser.pause_recording()
# These actions are NOT recorded
browser.snapshot()
browser.screenshot("setup.png")
browser.resume_recording()

browser.click_by_text("Continue")
browser.stop_recording()

docs.title: Recording Format
docs.text:
Recordings are saved as standard JSON files.

{
  "version": "1.0",
  "quay_version": "0.4.1",
  "recorded_at": "2026-03-24T12:00:00.000000",
  "actions": [
    {
      "type": "goto",
      "timestamp": 0.0,
      "url": "https://example.com"
    },
    {
      "type": "type_by_name",
      "timestamp": 1.25,
      "name": "username",
      "text": "REDACTED"
    }
  ],
  "metadata": {
    "total_duration": 1.25,
    "action_count": 2
  }
}

docs.title: Verification Mode
docs.text:
When verify=True is passed to playback(), the browser will check the return value of every action. If an action fails, a BrowserError is raised.

When verify=False (default), playback will continue even if some actions fail.

docs.title: Visual Regression Testing
docs.text:
Quay supports visual regression testing through pixel-by-pixel screenshot comparison.

⚠️ Basic Implementation

Screenshot comparison uses byte-by-byte comparison. No perceptual diff or threshold-based comparison yet. For advanced visual testing, consider integrating with tools like Percy or Applitools.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Visual comparison requires the compare extra dependencies:

pip install quay[compare]

Or install Pillow and numpy manually.

docs.title: Basic Comparison
docs.text:
from quay import Browser

browser = Browser()
result = browser.compare_screenshots("baseline.png", "current.png")

if result.match:
    print("Screenshots match exactly!")
else:
    print(f"Differences detected: {result.diff_percentage:.2f}%")
    print(f"Total differing pixels: {result.diff_pixels}")

docs.title: Tolerance Thresholds
docs.text:
UI rendering can vary slightly. Set a threshold (0.0 to 100.0) to allow a certain percentage of differing pixels.

# Allow up to 0.5% difference
result = browser.assert_visual_match("baseline.png", threshold=0.5)

docs.title: Visual Diffs
docs.text:
When a comparison fails, use output_diff to generate a diff image where differences are highlighted in red.

result = browser.compare_screenshots(
    "baseline.png",
    "current.png",
    output_diff="diff.png"
)

docs.title: Partial Comparison (Regions)
docs.text:
Compare a specific part of the page using the region parameter.

# Compare a 500x400 area starting at (100, 100)
result = browser.compare_screenshots(
    "baseline.png",
    "current.png",
    region=(100, 100, 500, 400)
)

docs.title: CI/CD Workflow
docs.text:
def test_dashboard_visual():
    browser = Browser()
    browser.goto("https://myapp.com/dashboard")

    # This will raise AssertionError if they don't match
    browser.assert_visual_match("tests/baselines/dashboard.png", threshold=0.1)

docs.title: Updating Baselines
docs.text:
# Update baseline with current view
browser.assert_visual_match("tests/baselines/dashboard.png", update_baseline=True)

docs.title: Comparison Metrics
docs.text: Property Description match True if diff_percentage <= threshold diff_pixels Total count of changed pixels diff_percentage Percentage of changed pixels baseline_size Tuple of (width, height) for baseline current_size Tuple of (width, height) for current diff_path Path to the generated diff image message Human-readable summary

docs.title: Wait Conditions
docs.text: Quay provides flexible wait conditions for handling dynamic content.

docs.title: wait_for()
docs.text:
The most versatile wait method. Accepts multiple keyword arguments:

# Wait for element to exist
browser.wait_for(selector="#loading")

# Wait for text to appear
browser.wait_for(text="Welcome")

# Wait for URL
browser.wait_for(url="https://example.com/success")

docs.title: wait_for_load_state()
docs.text:
Wait for specific page load states:

# Wait for page to fully load
browser.wait_for_load_state("complete")

# Wait for DOM ready
browser.wait_for_load_state("DOMContentLoaded")

# Wait for network idle
browser.wait_for_load_state("networkidle")

docs.title: wait_until()
docs.text:
Wait for custom condition:

# Wait for JavaScript condition
browser.wait_until(lambda: browser.evaluate("document.readyState") == "complete")

# Wait for element count
browser.wait_until(lambda: len(browser.accessibility_tree().find_by_role("button")) > 3)

docs.title: Timeout Configuration
docs.text:
# Default: 30 seconds
browser = Browser(timeout=30.0)

# Per-operation timeout
browser.wait_for(text="Done", timeout=5.0)
browser.wait_for(url="https://success", timeout=60.0)

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
from quay.errors import TimeoutError

try:
    browser.wait_for(text="Success", timeout=10.0)
except TimeoutError:
    print("Timed out waiting for Success")

docs.title: Wait for Page Load
docs.text:
browser.navigate("https://example.com")
browser.wait_for_load_state("complete")

docs.title: Wait for Element
docs.text:
browser.click("Load More")
browser.wait_for(selector=".results .item:nth-child(10)")

docs.title: Wait for Navigation
docs.text:
browser.click_by_text("Submit")
browser.wait_for(url="https://example.com/dashboard")

docs.title: Wait for AJAX
docs.text:
browser.click("Refresh")
browser.wait_for(text="Updated: ")

docs.title: Quay Architecture
docs.text: Quay uses a mixin-based architecture for maintainability and modularity.

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
The Browser class is composed of 8 focused mixins:

quay/
├── browser.py              # Main Browser class (41 lines)
├── _browser_core.py        # Connection lifecycle, HTTP helpers
├── _browser_tabs.py        # Tab CRUD operations
├── _browser_cdp.py         # Chrome DevTools Protocol low-level
├── _browser_navigation.py  # Navigation methods
├── _browser_wait.py        # Wait methods (8 variants)
├── _browser_accessibility.py  # Accessibility tree parsing
├── _browser_actions.py     # Click, type, screenshot, evaluate
├── _browser_recording.py   # Session recording & playback
├── connection.py           # WebSocket connection management
├── models.py               # Data models (Tab, AXNode, etc.)
└── errors.py               # Exception classes

docs.title: Mixin Composition
docs.text:
class Browser(
    BrowserCoreMixin,        # Base: __init__, start, stop, connect
    BrowserTabsMixin,        # Tab management
    BrowserCDPMixin,         # Low-level CDP methods
    BrowserRecordingMixin,   # Session recording
    BrowserNavigationMixin,  # Navigation helpers
    BrowserWaitMixin,        # Wait methods
    BrowserAccessibilityMixin,  # Accessibility tree
    BrowserActionsMixin,     # User actions
):
    pass

docs.title: Mixin Responsibilities
docs.text: Mixin Lines Purpose BrowserCoreMixin 229 Connection pooling, HTTP helpers, _run_async, _get_connection BrowserTabsMixin 101 list_tabs, new_tab, close_tab, activate_tab BrowserCDPMixin 45 _send_cdp, low-level CDP communication BrowserNavigationMixin 33 navigate, goto BrowserWaitMixin 109 wait_for, wait_for_url, wait_for_selector_* BrowserAccessibilityMixin 73 accessibility_tree, find_by_ref, find_by_role BrowserActionsMixin 122 click_by_text, type_text, evaluate, screenshot BrowserRecordingMixin 76 start_recording, stop_recording, playback

docs.title: Why Mixins?
docs.text:
1. Modularity: Each feature area is isolated in its own file
  2. Testability: Mixins can be tested independently
  3. Readability: browser.py is 41 lines instead of 3,000+
  4. Extensibility: Add new features by adding new mixins

docs.title: Type Hints with Mixins
docs.text:
Mixins access methods from other mixins they'll be combined with. Python's type system doesn't understand this by default, so we use:

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from ._browser_core import BrowserCoreMixin

class BrowserWaitMixin:
    def wait_for(self, selector=None, text=None, tab=None, timeout=10.0) -> bool:
        # Type ignore for mixin cross-references
        resolved_tab = self._resolve_tab(tab)  # type: ignore[attr-defined]

docs.title: Public API Unchanged
docs.text:
All public methods remain on the Browser class:

from quay import Browser

browser = Browser(port=9222)
browser.navigate("https://example.com")
browser.wait_for_selector_visible("#content")
tree = browser.accessibility_tree()

docs.title: Before vs After
docs.text:
Metric Before After browser.py lines 3,151 41 Total mixin lines — 788 Files 1 9 Max file size 116KB 8KB

The refactoring reduced the monolithic browser.py from an unmaintainable 3,151 lines to a simple 41-line composition class, with all functionality preserved in focused mixin modules.

docs.title: Reconnection Design
docs.text: This document defines the strategy for WebSocket reconnection and command retry logic.

docs.title: 1. Trigger Events
docs.text:
• WebSocket Close/Error: connection drops or error event
  • Send/Recv Timeout: command exceeds timeout without response
  • Health Check Failure: periodic health_check fails

docs.title: 2. State Preservation
docs.text: During reconnection: - Active Tab References: tab_id and metadata persist - Pending Operations: commands not timed out should be retried - Registered Interceptors: must be re-registered on new socket

docs.title: 3. Connection Behavior
docs.text:
• Queueing: new calls to _send_cdp() queue while reconnecting
  • Retry Logic: failed commands retried automatically once
  • Configurability: max_retries and reconnect_delay user-configurable

docs.title: State Machine
docs.text:
[CONNECTING] --(Success)--> [HEALTHY] <--(Command Send)
     ^                         |  ^
     |                         |  +------------+
  (Retry)                      |
     |                   (Error/Timeout/Close)
     |                         |
     +------- [RECONNECTING] <--+
                    |
               (Max Retries)
                    |
              [FAILED/CLOSED]

docs.title: Connection Updates
docs.text:
• ensure_connected(retry_count): orchestrate reconnection
  • reconnect(): close socket and call connect()

docs.title: Browser Updates
docs.text:
• reconnect_retries: int = 3
  • reconnect_delay: float = 1.0

docs.title: New Error Handling
docs.text:
• ReconnectionError: raised when max_retries exceeded
  • PermanentFailure: raised when Chrome unreachable

docs.title: Strategy for Retrying Operations
docs.text:
1. If Connection.send() fails with network exception or timeout:
  2. Mark Connection as RECONNECTING
  3. Perform reconnection sequence (disconnect, connect, health-check)
  4. If successful, re-send original command with original id
  5. If reconnect fails after max_retries, cancel pending Future with original exception

docs.title: Quay — Specification
docs.text: Chrome DevTools with accessibility-tree semantics for browser automation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Quay connects to Chrome via the Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP), enabling browser automation that preserves your logged-in sessions (Gmail, GitHub, banking, SSO). Unlike Selenium/Playwright which spin up fresh browser instances, Quay uses Chrome you already have running with --remote-debugging-port=9222.

docs.title: Key Properties
docs.text: Property Value Language Python >=3.10 Dependencies websockets >= 12.0 only (stdlib + websockets) Package src/quay/ Entry point quay CLI / from quay import Browser License MIT

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Quay is organized into layers:

  • Python API (browser.py) and CLI (cli.py)
  • CDP Client Layer (HTTP for tab management, WebSocket for commands)
  • Chrome DevTools Protocol (your Chrome instance)

docs.title: Browser(host, port, timeout)
docs.text:
Main browser automation class. Raises ConnectionError if Chrome is not reachable.

from quay import Browser

browser = Browser()
browser = Browser(port=9223)

docs.title: list_tabs()
docs.text: Returns list of all open tabs.

docs.title: new_tab(url)
docs.text: Opens a new tab and navigates to URL.

docs.title: activate_tab(tab_id)
docs.text: Brings tab to front.

docs.title: close_tab(tab_id)
docs.text: Closes specified tab or current tab.

docs.title: current_tab()
docs.text: Returns the currently active tab.

docs.title: get_version()
docs.text: Returns Chrome version and protocol info.

docs.title: navigate(url, tab=None)
docs.text: Navigates current tab (or specified tab) to URL.

docs.title: goto(url)
docs.text: Convenience: creates new tab, navigates, waits 0.5s for initial load.

docs.title: wait_for(selector, text, url, timeout)
docs.text: Polls until element selector, text content, or URL appears.

docs.title: wait_for_load_state(state)
docs.text: Wait for page load state: "load", "DOMContentLoaded", "networkidle".

docs.title: wait_for_function(script)
docs.text: Wait for JavaScript expression to return truthy value.

docs.title: accessibility_tree(tab=None)
docs.text:
Returns AXNode tree. Tree uses Chrome nodeId as ref for element targeting.

tree = browser.accessibility_tree()
links = tree.find_by_role("link")
buttons = tree.find_by_role("button")
node = tree.find("42")

docs.title: click(ref, tab, timeout, double, button)
docs.text: Click element by accessibility ref.

docs.title: click_by_text(text, tab, timeout)
docs.text: Click first element matching visible text.

docs.title: type_text(ref, text, tab, timeout)
docs.text: Type text into element by ref.

docs.title: type_by_name(name, text, tab, timeout)
docs.text: Type by accessible name (label, placeholder, aria-label).

docs.title: type_slowly(ref, text, delay, tab, timeout)
docs.text: Type character by character.

docs.title: press_key(key, modifiers, tab, timeout)
docs.text: Press key or key combination.

docs.title: hover(ref, tab, timeout)
docs.text: Hover over element by ref.

docs.title: fill_form(fields, tab, timeout)
docs.text: Fill multiple form fields by label/placeholder/name.

docs.title: screenshot(path, tab, timeout)
docs.text: Capture PNG screenshot.

docs.title: get_html(tab)
docs.text: Returns page outerHTML.

docs.title: evaluate(script, tab, timeout)
docs.text: Execute JavaScript and return result.

docs.title: get_cookies(urls, tab)
docs.text: Get all cookies or filtered by URLs.

docs.title: set_cookies(cookies, tab)
docs.text: Set one or more cookies.

docs.title: delete_cookies(name, url, tab)
docs.text: Delete specific or all cookies.

docs.title: on_request(callback, tab)
docs.text: Register callback for network requests.

docs.title: on_response(callback, tab)
docs.text: Register callback for network responses.

docs.title: on_request_failed(callback, tab)
docs.text: Register callback for failed requests.

docs.text: Clear all registered handlers.

docs.title: Exception Hierarchy
docs.text:
BrowserError (base)
├── ConnectionError
├── TimeoutError
├── TabError
└── CDPError

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text:
Usage: quay command [args]

Command Args Description list - List all open tabs new url Open new tab snapshot - Print accessibility tree screenshot path Save screenshot html - Print page HTML eval js Execute JavaScript click text Click element by text navigate url Navigate current tab close id Close tabs version - Show Chrome version info

docs.title: Implementation Status (v0.2.6)
docs.text: All core features implemented: tab management, navigation, accessibility tree, click/type, forms, cookies, network interception, CLI, type hints, tests.

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
1. Localhost only — CDP debugging on 9222 is localhost-only by default
  2. Arbitrary JS execution — evaluate() runs whatever you pass
  3. No sandbox — Quay uses your real Chrome session
  4. No authentication — Anyone with localhost CDP access can automate your browser

docs.title: Why accessibility tree?
docs.text: Semantic targeting (by role, name, label) instead of CSS selectors. Stable across page restructuring.

docs.title: Why WebSockets for commands?
docs.text: CDP commands (Page.navigate, Accessibility.getFullAXTree, Runtime.evaluate) require bidirectional communication.

docs.title: Why stdlib + websockets only?
docs.text:
Minimal dependency surface.

Last updated: 2026-03-24

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Quay requires a browser with remote debugging enabled. This creates unique testing challenges.

docs.title: 1. Unit Tests (Mocked)
docs.text:
Purpose: Test internal logic without Chrome. Location: tests/test_*.py Run: pytest tests/ -v Status: All passing (105 tests) Coverage: AXNode parsing, error classes, connection pool logic, wait methods, recording, design fix tests

Test modules: - tests/test_models.py — Tab, AXNode, Action, Recording, ComparisonResult - tests/test_errors.py — BrowserError hierarchy and context - tests/test_escape.py — JavaScript string escaping security - tests/test_actions.py — Key definitions and input handling - tests/test_accessibility.py — AXNode parsing and search - tests/test_connection.py — OperationQueue, ConnectionState - tests/test_wait.py — Wait method signatures and async polling - tests/test_browser.py — Browser composition tests - tests/test_navigation.py — Navigation and tab methods - tests/test_recording.py — Recording save/load and playback whitelist - tests/test_cli.py — CLI command tests - tests/test_design_fixes.py — Tests for design issue fixes (DES-1 through DES-6)

docs.title: 2. Integration Tests (Real Chrome)
docs.text: Purpose: Test against actual Chrome DevTools Protocol. Location: Tests with @pytest.mark.integration Run: pytest tests/ -m integration Requirement: Chrome with --remote-debugging-port=9222 Status: Skipped by default

docs.title: 3. Smoke Tests (Quick Validation)
docs.text: Purpose: Fast check that core functionality works. Location: tests/test_smoke.py Run: pytest tests/test_smoke.py -v

docs.title: 4. Live Tests (Full Workflow)
docs.text: Purpose: End-to-end scenarios. Location: tests/test_live.py Run: pytest tests/test_live.py -v --live
